findings.title: StrengthDto.value typed as Number serializes Doubles as decimals (e.g. 18738.0), violating contract literal 18738
findings.suggested_fix: Frontend contract shows `"value": 18738` (no decimal). However, SeasonMetrics fields are all Double? and StrengthSelector passes those Double values into StrengthDto(metric, v, ...). Jackson serializes a Double-backed Number as `18738.0`, not `18738`. Either: (a) change StrengthDto.value to Double and update the contract example to `18738.0` for AVG_ORDER_AMOUNT/TABLE_REVENUE/etc, or (b) round to Long when the metric is monetary and keep Double for ratio metrics (TURNOVER_RATE, LOW_CANCELLATION_RATE) — but that requires a richer DTO. The current `Number` type provides no serialization stability; the actual JSON shape is determined by the runtime subtype, which silently breaks frontend parsers expecting integers.

findings.title: Strength metric identifiers are hardcoded strings instead of enum-backed, risking typos and silent contract drift
findings.suggested_fix: Persona instructions reference a `SeasonMetricType` enum, but no such enum exists in the repo. The literals `"AVG_ORDER_AMOUNT"`, `"TURNOVER_RATE"`, `"TABLE_REVENUE"`, `"LOW_CANCELLATION_RATE"` are duplicated as raw strings across StrengthSelector and frontend contract. Introduce an enum `SeasonMetricType { AVG_ORDER_AMOUNT, TURNOVER_RATE, TABLE_REVENUE, LOW_CANCELLATION_RATE }` and either (a) type StrengthDto.metric as that enum (Jackson will serialize via name()), or (b) use `SeasonMetricType.AVG_ORDER_AMOUNT.name` at the call sites. Today, a typo in any of these literals would silently ship a wrong contract value with no compiler help.

findings.title: MilestoneStateDto.currentTier null is serialized rather than omitted; contract example does not show null branch shape
findings.suggested_fix: Contract states currentTier may be null. With Spring/Jackson defaults (no global JsonInclude.NON_NULL detected in /global/configuration), the response will include `"currentTier": null` rather than omitting the key. Confirm with frontend whether the agreed shape is the explicit-null form (current) or the omitted form. If omitted is desired, annotate the field with @JsonInclude(JsonInclude.Include.NON_NULL) or set a global default. Either choice is fine, but it must be explicit and locked by a serialization test.

findings.title: businessDayCount typed as Long; contract example shows small integer with no obvious need for 64-bit width
findings.suggested_fix: JSON does not distinguish Int vs Long, so a Long of 2 serializes as `2` and is contract-compatible. Flagging only because Long for a business-day counter is unusual; a TS frontend will receive a `number` either way. No action required unless the field originates from a count(*) returning long — in which case keep as-is. No contract break.

findings.title: No global ControllerAdvice / error response schema present; new endpoint inherits Spring defaults that may diverge from rest of API
findings.suggested_fix: A repo-wide grep for @ControllerAdvice / @ExceptionHandler returned no results, which means errors thrown from workspaceService.checkAccessible (e.g. access denied) will surface with whatever default shape Spring Boot produces (`{timestamp,status,error,path}`). If other admin endpoints already document a different error envelope (e.g. `{message}` or `{errors:[...]}`), the new season-insight endpoint will be inconsistent. Confirm the agreed error envelope with frontend and ensure the endpoint produces the same shape on 401/403/404.

residual_risks: StrengthDto.value's runtime numeric subtype is determined at construction site; future changes to SeasonMetrics field types (e.g., Double -> Long) will silently change wire format.
No serialization (MockMvc/JSON snapshot) test was found locking the response shape, so any DTO field rename or enum name change would compile cleanly and only break the frontend at runtime.
Hardcoded metric literals in StrengthSelector are not protected by tests against typos.
testing_gaps: Add a MockMvc test that asserts JSON keys exactly match contract: cumulativeSales, businessDayCount, strengths[].metric, strengths[].value, strengths[].percentile, socialProof.topSales, socialProof.topAvgOrderAmount, socialProof.topTurnoverRate, milestone.currentTier.
Add a serialization test for milestone.currentTier == null to lock either the present-null or omitted-key form.
Add a test that ensures strengths is `[]` (not null/absent) when distribution.populationSize is 0.
Add an enum-name regression test asserting MilestoneTier.values().map { it.name } == ["TOP_50","TOP_30","TOP_10"] and that StrengthSelector emits exactly the four metric strings in the contract.
Add a contract-level assertion that StrengthDto.value serializes without a decimal point if it is meant to be an integer (the current Double-backed Number will fail this).
